resource_contract.detail: A fixed corpus proves typed detector and observable limits, target construction, value semantics, canonical text, standalone parsing, and the explicit numeric shift-target boundary.
Fixed-size instructions prove every selected kind, constructor, accessor, value contract, canonical rendering, tag, probability, coordinate, target-role, separator, and exact-target-count rule.
Fixed-size empty, single, and separator-delimited target lists prove grouping semantics without a resource claim.

A fixed corpus proves comments, case normalization, whitespace, CRLF input, tags, escaped tags, repeats, every instruction kind, canonical printing, parse-print-parse equality, and representative malformed syntax.

A three-item model proves default and explicit construction, value semantics, instruction and repeat insertion, item access and ranges, atomic text append, clear, repeat accessors, and canonical rendering.
A compact repeated model proves detector shift, detector count, observable count, error count, coordinate shift, and checked detector-shift overflow without flattening.
A fixed repeated model proves all-detector, selected-detector, and single-detector coordinate queries plus shifted coordinate accumulation and out-of-range rejection.
The generated distance-five, seven-round surface-code case proves folded DEM coordinates terminate and exactly match independently queried circuit coordinates.

A small repeated model proves adjusted detector and coordinate shifts for lazy and materialized flattening; a trillion-repeat model proves bounded lazy prefixes while materialized expansion fails closed.
A compact nested model proves probability-only rounding, zero retention, recursive tag removal, repeat preservation, and source immutability.
One exact structural selector binds the imported basic, repeat, and selected Rust model API rows to the independently owned model value and mutation contract.
The exact structural selector binds the imported generated surface-code coordinate row while the independent canonical parent owns the upstream nontermination regression.
One exact aggregate selector binds the imported C++ and Python DEM-instruction fixture rows to the independently owned target, validation, print, and grouping contracts.
The exact imported PF1 selector proves repeated error counting plus all, selected, and single-detector coordinate queries with empty defaults and out-of-range rejection.
One exact aggregate selector binds the imported transform row to independent flattening, rounding, zero-retention, tag-removal, repeat-preservation, and expansion-limit contracts.
One exact aggregate selector binds the imported validation row to typed-id, target, instruction, parser, repeat, tag, and non-finite-coordinate rejection contracts.
The exact imported resource selector proves a full coordinate map rejects more than one million materialized detectors while selected and single-detector queries fold through the same repeat.
This case proves canonical parse-print-parse behavior; parser admission and resource limits have independent CQ owners.

A fixed corpus proves accepted whitespace, aliases, comments, fusion, repeats, record and Pauli targets plus malformed gate, argument, target, and record forms; parser-wide admission limits have independent CQ owners.
This case proves canonical printing of aliases, plain and classical pair targets, measurement targets, detector and observable records, probabilities, and Pauli lists.
Fixed-size instructions prove sweep controls are accepted in the controlled position and rejected by single-qubit gates.
This case proves simple, empty, escaped, and canonical tags plus preservation of nested repeat-block structure without a resource claim.
This case proves MPP product grouping and optional measurement probability canonicalization without a resource claim.
This case proves SPP and SPP_DAG Pauli-product parsing and canonical printing without a resource claim.
This case proves successful typed circuit file input and canonical output; path errors and size admission have separate cases.
This case proves canonical text append behavior across tags, repeats, and fusion boundaries without a resource claim.
This case proves the public append_from_stim_program_text alias delegates to canonical append semantics.
This case proves construction and extraction boundaries for typed negative measurement-record offsets.
This case proves accepted and rejected qubit, record, sweep, and Pauli target text boundaries.
This case proves typed extraction from qubit, Pauli, record, sweep, and combiner targets.
This case proves invertible result targets and rejection of combiner, record, and sweep inversion.
This case proves classical, Pauli, qubit, result, and combiner target classification.
Fixed-size malformed repeat grammar is rejected without mutating an existing circuit, including zero counts, missing counts, extra braces, and non-repeat braces.
Fixed-size typed argument and target vectors prove pair cardinality, duplicate-target, Pauli-role, inversion, and probability validation before instruction construction.
Fixed-size one- and two-qubit Pauli-channel inputs prove exact list arity, closed-unit entries, disjoint sums, pair cardinality, and typed NaN rejection.
This case proves recursive tag removal while preserving repeat structure and instruction arguments.
Fixed-size circuits prove canonical fusion, non-fusion, invalid append rejection, and fusion across a concatenation boundary.
Each fixed-size syntax record is parsed into a bounded materialized circuit or rejected before a circuit is returned; parser-wide size and nesting limits have independent CQ owners.
The fixed-size input remains compact while proving large-repeat parsing, finite signed coordinates, exponent rejection, and CRLF equivalence without expanding the repeat.
This case proves empty, singleton, pair, Pauli-product, measurement-pad, and coordinate target grouping and constructs every non-REPEAT gate shape.
A fixed-size typed instruction proves canonical printing and eager pair-target cardinality validation.
This fixed corpus proves typed instruction construction, accessors, cloning, and value equality across differing gates, arguments, and targets.

static_property_plan.generator_domain: fixed CircuitInstruction constructor, accessor, clone, and equality variants

resource_contract.detail: This fixed corpus proves zero, pair-measurement, per-target measurement, and measurement-pad count semantics.

static_property_plan.generator_domain: fixed instruction measurement-count gate and target-shape variants

resource_contract.detail: A fixed value corpus proves both public SampleFormat paths, all selected variants, copy and clone behavior, debug names, and equality.
Exact fixed records prove 01, b8, hits, dets, and r8 writer bytes plus writer construction, value semantics, result-type transitions, and extraction.
Exact records prove empty and repeated dets boundaries, long r8 gaps, and non-byte-aligned bit-slice writes.
A five-shot table proves shot-major batch writing in every selected non-PTB64 format and the exact 64-shot PTB64 measurement-major word layout.
A 100-bit reference-sample table proves exact sparse, packed, run-length, typed dets, and PTB64 bytes including one shot-local flip.
The exact 102-measurement sequence proves append, lookback, unwritten output, compaction, storage reporting, cloning, equality, and debug behavior.
Fixed scalar and batch histories prove zero, available, beginning-of-time, and configured maximum lookback boundaries.
The pinned 1102-measurement five-shot sequence proves intermediate 256-row flushing, final output, written cursors, lookback retention, and compaction.
A fixed five-shot history proves editable zero rows, width rejection, lookback values, cloning, equality, and debug behavior.
A 300-measurement regression proves complete chunks flush once, reference bits use global measurement indices, retained storage stays bounded, and final output appends only the remainder.
A two-shot fixed record proves a short reference sample is measurement-indexed and zero-padded instead of being interpreted as one reference bit per shot.
Pinned 18-bit records prove materialized, dense streaming, packed streaming, and sparse streaming decoding for 01, b8, hits, and r8.
Deterministic 64, 128, 256, and 504-bit multi-record corpora prove all selected non-PTB64 formats through materialized, dense, packed, and sparse APIs.
Exact multi-record inputs prove final-line handling, empty dets records, measurement-only dets typing, blank-line handling, and CRLF acceptance.
A circuit-derived detector and observable layout proves typed D/L offsets and every 01, b8, r8, hits, and dets conversion direction.
Typed dets input proves detector and observable namespaces stay distinct through observable index 1000 and flatten into the declared layout exactly.
Positive integer parsing and exact negative cases prove width, byte, run-length, sparse-index, u64-overflow, dets-prefix, and token validation in every reader family.
Repeated hits and dets tokens prove dense materialized, dense streaming, and packed streaming readers toggle duplicate indices.
Repeated and out-of-order sparse tokens prove sparse visitors preserve the source token sequence without sorting or deduplication.
Two 64-shot groups of 71-bit records prove PTB64 layout, inferred counts, bounded prefixes, dense and sparse reconstruction, and exact group, width, and truncation failures.
A deterministic 576 by 1000-bit table proves exact write/read round trips across all selected formats, including nine complete PTB64 groups.
Every public streaming visitor family stops after its first callback error without parsing or visiting a later record.
Fixed sparse shots prove public fields, construction, cloning, equality, debug output, Stim-style text, and low-64 observable mask semantics.
Circuit-derived measurement layout converts exact 01, b8, hits, and r8 records to typed dets output.
Circuit-derived detector and observable records prove primary typed dets output and exact observable side-output routing.
Explicit measurement, detector, and observable counts plus DEM-derived layouts prove every selected non-PTB64 conversion direction.
Measurement layouts and raw bits-per-shot mode prove every selected non-PTB64 format plus an exact 2048-bit b8 record.
Exact CLI failures prove circuit types are mandatory and unique, layout information is required, and raw bits cannot be emitted as typed dets.
Typed CLI dets conversion toggles repeated tokens using the same parity semantics as Stim dense readers.
This exact structural selector binds the imported writer coverage row to independently owned exact format, boundary, bit-slice, and table contracts.
This exact structural selector binds the imported reader coverage row to independently owned exact format, width, malformed-input, typed-layout, and PTB64 contracts.
This exact structural selector binds the imported scalar measurement-record row to the independently owned state, lookback, output, and compaction contracts.
This exact structural selector binds the imported batch-record row to independently owned chunking, reference, lookback, editable-zero, and compaction contracts.
This exact structural selector binds the imported batch-writer row to the independently owned shot-major and PTB64 table contracts.
This exact structural selector binds the imported sparse-shot row to the independently owned value, text, field, and observable-mask contracts.
This exact structural selector binds the imported convert coverage row to independently owned circuit, DEM, explicit-layout, raw-width, PTB64, side-output, and failure contracts.
